name: Spodumene

photos.locality: Pala Chief mine, San Diego County, California — gem-grade kunzite variety

identification.colour: White to grey; gem varieties: pale green (hiddenite), lavender to pink (kunzite)
identification.lustre: Vitreous to pearly on cleavage faces
identification.crystal_habit: Prismatic, typically flattened; strongly striated lengthwise along c-axis; often coarse and massive in ore deposits

canada.geological_setting: Found exclusively in lithium-caesium-tantalum (LCT) pegmatites — late-crystallising granitic bodies enriched in incompatible elements. Canada's most significant resources are at Patriot Battery Metals' Shaakichiuwaanaan deposit in Quebec's James Bay territory (one of the largest hard-rock lithium resources in the Americas), the Yellowknife pegmatite province in the Northwest Territories, and emerging projects near Val-d'Or, QC. British Columbia hosts spodumene in the Shuswap Metamorphic Complex.

handling: No significant chemical hazards. As with all silicate minerals, avoid prolonged inhalation of fine dust. Crystals are brittle on two well-developed cleavage planes; handle with care to avoid sharp edges.
known_for: The primary ore mineral driving the global EV battery revolution. Spodumene concentrate is processed into lithium hydroxide for cathode manufacture. Canada's James Bay spodumene resources are positioned to serve North American gigafactories under the Canada-US Critical Minerals Agreement. The mineral was first described in 1800 from a Swedish pegmatite; its economic importance was negligible until the electrification era.
gem_note: Kunzite (pink to lilac) and hiddenite (emerald-green) are highly sought gem varieties. Both display strong trichroism — colour shifts dramatically depending on viewing angle. Kunzite crystals from large pegmatites can exceed 1,000 carats in cut weight. The colour of kunzite fades with prolonged UV exposure.

name: Uraninite

identification.colour: Pitch-black to grey-black; streak: black to brownish-black
identification.lustre: Greasy to submetallic; massive pitchblende form is dull

hardness.plain: Moderately hard — will scratch glass with effort

identification.crystal_habit: Cubic crystals (rare); most commonly massive, botryoidal, or colloform (pitchblende form)

canada.geological_setting: In Saskatchewan's Athabasca Basin, uraninite occurs at unconformity-type deposits where uranium-bearing hydrothermal fluids were focused along the contact between Proterozoic sandstone and older basement rocks. McArthur River, Cigar Lake, and Key Lake have ore grades 10–100× higher than any other deposit type in the world. In Ontario's Elliot Lake and Bancroft areas, uraninite occurs in Archean conglomerate reefs and pegmatites.

handling: RADIOACTIVE — uraninite emits alpha, beta, and gamma radiation. Handle only with proper radiation safety protocols: minimise exposure time, maximise distance, use shielding. Do not handle without training. Specimens intended for display should be stored in sealed containers. Radon gas, a decay product, can accumulate in enclosed spaces. Consult your jurisdiction's radiation safety guidelines before acquiring specimens.
known_for: The primary ore mineral of uranium — the fuel for nuclear power and a feedstock for defence applications. Saskatchewan's Athabasca Basin is the world's highest-grade uranium jurisdiction, accounting for roughly 22% of global primary uranium production. The mineral pitchblende (massive uraninite) was studied by Marie and Pierre Curie, who discovered radium and polonium within it in 1898. Canada was the world's largest uranium producer for most of the 20th century.

name: Cobaltite

identification.colour: Silver-white to greyish-white, often with a reddish tinge; tarnishes to grey

hardness.plain: Moderately hard — roughly the hardness of a steel knife blade

identification.crystal_habit: Cubic, pyritohedric, or octahedral crystals; also massive and granular

canada.geological_setting: In Ontario's Cobalt camp (Temiskaming Highlands), cobaltite occurs in five-element vein deposits — a distinctive hydrothermal deposit type containing cobalt, nickel, arsenic, silver, and bismuth minerals in carbonate veins cross-cutting Proterozoic diabase sills and Archean metasediments. The camp produced over 600 million troy ounces of silver between 1903 and the 1980s. Modern interest focuses on processing historic tailings for battery-grade cobalt and refinery development at Temiskaming Shores.

handling: Contains arsenic — a cumulative toxin. Do not grind, heat, or generate dust without ventilation and respiratory protection. Wash hands thoroughly after handling. Specimens with visible pink erythrite (cobalt bloom) surface oxidation should be treated with particular care, as erythrite is more soluble than cobaltite. Store away from food and children.
known_for: The mineral that gave Cobalt, Ontario its name and made it one of the richest mining camps in North American history. The silver boom of the early 1900s built the Canadian banking industry and financed expansion of northern Ontario's rail network. Today cobalt is strategic for lithium-ion battery cathodes (NMC and NCA chemistry), and Canada is working to reactivate the Cobalt camp's refining infrastructure.

name: Pentlandite

identification.colour: Bronze-yellow to light bronze-brown; streak: light bronze-brown

hardness.plain: Relatively soft — a copper coin will scratch it

identification.crystal_habit: Massive; rarely as distinct grains; commonly intergrown with pyrrhotite as characteristic flame-textured lamellae visible in polished section

canada.geological_setting: Occurs in magmatic nickel-copper-PGE sulphide deposits associated with mafic and ultramafic intrusions. The Sudbury Basin (Ontario) — formed by a 1.85 billion-year-old meteorite impact that melted and differentiated crustal rocks — hosts one of the world's largest nickel-copper sulphide deposits. Vale's Sudbury operations and Glencore's Sudbury Integrated Nickel Operations produce nickel, copper, cobalt, and platinum-group elements. The Thompson Nickel Belt in Manitoba is a world-class magmatic system hosted in Proterozoic komatiites.

handling: No significant chemical hazards under normal handling. Fine dust should not be inhaled (general mineral precaution). Note that pentlandite is commonly associated with pyrrhotite, which on oxidation can generate sulphuric acid in mine drainage contexts.
known_for: The primary ore mineral of nickel — one of Canada's most economically significant metals. Sudbury's nickel has been mined continuously since 1883 and continues to supply global stainless steel, battery, and aerospace markets. Nickel's role in high-density lithium-ion cathodes (NMC 811 chemistry) has renewed global demand. Canada is among the few countries with large-scale Class 1 nickel refining capacity outside China.

name: Graphite

photos.locality: Minas Gerais, Brazil — large crystalline flakes

identification.colour: Steel-grey to black; streak: black, shiny and smears
identification.lustre: Metallic to dull; distinctly greasy feel

hardness.plain: Very soft — marks paper like a pencil (it is the pencil)

identification.crystal_habit: Platy hexagonal flakes (flake graphite); massive (amorphous graphite); vein or lump graphite in metamorphic veins

canada.geological_setting: Canadian graphite occurs mainly as crystalline flake graphite in Precambrian marbles, gneisses, and schists formed by metamorphism of organic carbon-rich sediments. Key deposits include Zenyatta's Albany graphite in a nepheline syenite pipe (northern Ontario) and Nouveau Monde Graphite's Matawinie project near Montreal, QC — one of the largest natural flake graphite resources in the Americas. Quebec's Outaouais region hosts additional advanced-stage projects targeting battery anode markets.

handling: No significant toxicological hazard. Carbon dust is a nuisance dust; avoid prolonged inhalation of fine graphite powder in industrial settings. The greasy feel is characteristic and will transfer to hands and surfaces. Natural graphite is chemically inert and safe to handle without PPE for specimen purposes.
known_for: The only critical mineral that also writes. Natural flake graphite is the primary anode material in lithium-ion batteries — every EV cell contains approximately 10× more graphite than lithium by weight. Canada's flake graphite deposits are among the highest-purity natural resources globally, positioned to supply the North American battery supply chain. Graphite also moderates nuclear reactors, lubricates machinery at extreme temperatures, and is the structural basis of carbon fibre and graphene.
